rendered:
Hey Pooja!

Really excited to get you started on this journey. Recomposition is one of the most rewarding goals — you're going to build strength, change how your body looks and feels, and honestly, the consistency piece you mentioned struggling with? That's going to click once you start seeing what your body can do. Since you're working out at home with dumbbells and resistance bands, we're going to make every session count with smart exercise selection and solid progression. Three days a week is perfect for a beginner — enough to stimulate growth and strength, with plenty of recovery time built in.

We're running an Upper-Lower-Full Body split this week. It's a great structure for recomposition because you're hitting each muscle group multiple times across the week without overdoing it. You'll get stronger on the big movements, build some lean muscle, and keep your metabolism fired up. Let's make it happen.

## Warmup Routine (Do this before every training session — around 10 minutes)

Start with 3-4 minutes of light movement to get your heart rate up and blood flowing — marching in place, arm circles, leg swings, or a brisk walk around the house. Then move into some joint mobility: hip circles (10 each direction), arm circles (10 forward, 10 back), bodyweight squats (10-12 reps, slow and controlled), and some cat-cow stretches if your lower back feels tight. Finish with a few inchworms or standing toe touches to wake up the hamstrings and core.

Before you get into your main lifts, run through a couple of lighter warm-up sets — maybe 10-12 reps with about 50% of your working weight, then another set with 70-75%. This primes your nervous system and helps you nail your form when it counts.

---

## Day 1 — Lower Body Strength

**Goblet Squat** (holding a dumbbell at chest height)  
4 sets x 6 reps  
*Focus on sitting back into your hips, keeping your chest proud. Control the descent for 2-3 seconds, then drive up powerfully. This is one of your favorites, so let's make it a cornerstone. Rest 2-3 minutes between sets.*

**Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift** (dumbbell in one hand)  
3 sets x 8 reps per leg  
*Great for hamstrings, glutes, and balance. Keep a slight bend in your standing knee, hinge at the hips, and feel the stretch down the back of your leg. Don't rush these — quality over speed.*

**Reverse Lunges** (dumbbells at your sides)  
3 sets x 10 reps per leg  
*Step back, drop the back knee toward the floor, and push through the front heel to stand. Keep your torso upright. These are killer for glutes and quads.*

**Glute Bridge** (dumbbell resting on your hips)  
3 sets x 15 reps  
*Squeeze your glutes hard at the top and hold for a full second. This is an isolation move, so you can push closer to failure here.*

**Resistance Band Leg Curls** (lying face down, band around one ankle)  
3 sets x 12 reps per leg  
*Curl your heel toward your glutes, squeeze, and lower with control. Hamstrings respond well to higher reps.*

**Core Circuit** (do all three back-to-back, rest 60-90 seconds, repeat for 3 rounds)  
- Plank Hold: 30-45 seconds  
- Dead Bug: 10 reps per side (slow and controlled)  
- Bicycle Crunches: 15 reps per side

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 6,000-7,000 steps today. Since you're training in the evening, try to get a chunk of your steps in during the day — a short walk after lunch works wonders for digestion and energy.

---

## Day 2 — Active Recovery

No heavy lifting today. This is about keeping blood flowing, working on mobility, and letting your muscles recover. Spend 20-30 minutes doing some light movement — a walk around the neighborhood, gentle yoga, or some dynamic stretching. Focus on areas that feel tight from yesterday (hips, quads, hamstrings).

Here's a simple mobility flow you can do at home:  
- Cat-Cow Stretch: 10 reps  
- World's Greatest Stretch: 5 reps per side  
- 90/90 Hip Stretch: 60 seconds per side  
- Child's Pose: 60 seconds  
- Downward Dog to Cobra Flow: 8-10 reps

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 5,000-6,000 steps. Keep it light and easy — this is recovery, not a cardio session.

---

## Day 3 — Upper Body Strength

**Dumbbell Floor Press** (lying on your back)  
4 sets x 6 reps  
*This replaces a bench press and is fantastic for building pressing strength. Lower the dumbbells until your elbows touch the floor, pause for a second, then press up powerfully. Rest 2-3 minutes between sets.*

**Resistance Band Rows** (anchor the band at chest height or loop it around a sturdy post)  
4 sets x 8 reps  
*You mentioned loving rows, so let's make these count. Pull the band toward your ribcage, squeeze your shoulder blades together, and control the release. Think about pulling with your elbows, not your hands.*

**Dumbbell Shoulder Press** (standing or seated)  
3 sets x 8 reps  
*Press the dumbbells overhead until your arms are fully extended, then lower with control. Keep your core tight to protect your lower back.*

**Resistance Band Lat Pulldown** (anchor the band overhead, pull down to chest height)  
3 sets x 12 reps  
*Great for building that upper back width. Pull down and slightly back, squeezing your lats at the bottom.*

**Dumbbell Bicep Curl**  
3 sets x 12 reps  
*Strict form here — no swinging. Curl up, squeeze at the top, lower slowly. You can push the last set close to failure and add a couple of controlled cheat reps if you've got nothing left in the tank.*

**Resistance Band Tricep Extension** (anchor the band overhead, extend arms down)  
3 sets x 15 reps  
*Keep your elbows tucked and extend fully at the bottom. Triceps love higher reps, so don't be shy about feeling the burn.*

**Core Circuit** (3 rounds, rest 60-90 seconds between rounds)  
- Side Plank: 20-30 seconds per side  
- Russian Twists: 20 reps total (slow and controlled)  
- Leg Raises: 12 reps

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 6,000-7,000 steps. Since you're training in the evening, try to stay active during the day — even just standing and moving around your workspace helps.

---

## Day 4 — Rest Day

Full rest today. Your body needs time to repair and adapt. This is when the magic happens — muscle grows, strength builds, and your nervous system recharges. If you feel like moving, keep it super light — a 10-15 minute walk, some gentle stretching, or just enjoy the day off.

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 4,000-5,000 steps. No pressure — just normal daily movement.

---

## Day 5 — Full Body Hypertrophy

This session is all about building muscle and creating metabolic stress. We're using slightly higher reps, shorter rest periods, and a mix of compound and isolation work. You'll feel this one.

**Dumbbell Goblet Squat**  
3 sets x 12 reps  
*Same movement as Day 1, but higher reps and a bit lighter weight. Focus on tempo — 2 seconds down, 1 second pause at the bottom, explode up. Rest 90 seconds between sets.*

**Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift** (both legs, dumbbells at your sides)  
3 sets x 10 reps  
*Hinge at the hips, feel the stretch in your hamstrings, and drive through your heels to stand. Keep the dumbbells close to your legs throughout.*

**Dumbbell Floor Press**  
3 sets x 10 reps  
*Lighter than Day 3, but still challenging. Control the descent, pause at the bottom, press up strong.*

**Resistance Band Rows**  
3 sets x 15 reps  
*Pull hard, squeeze your shoulder blades, control the release. You can push these close to failure — it's an isolation move, so your form won't break down as easily.*

**Dumbbell Lateral Raise**  
3 sets x 15 reps  
*Lift the dumbbells out to the sides until they're at shoulder height, pause, lower slowly. Shoulders respond really well to higher reps and time under tension.*

**Resistance Band Glute Kickback**  
3 sets x 15 reps per leg  
*Loop the band around one ankle, kick back and up, squeeze your glute at the top. Keep your core tight so you're not arching your lower back.*

**Core Circuit** (3 rounds, rest 60-90 seconds between rounds)  
- Mountain Climbers: 20 reps total  
- Plank Shoulder Taps: 20 reps total  
- Reverse Crunches: 15 reps

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 6,000-7,000 steps. You're training in the evening, so front-load some steps during the day if you can.

---

## Day 6 — Active Recovery

Same as Day 2 — light movement, mobility work, and recovery. Your muscles have been working hard, and this day is about keeping them loose and promoting blood flow without adding stress.

Mobility flow suggestion:  
- Hip Flexor Stretch: 60 seconds per side  
- Pigeon Pose: 60 seconds per side  
- Thoracic Spine Rotations: 10 reps per side  
- Hamstring Stretch (seated or standing): 60 seconds per side  
- Shoulder Dislocates with a resistance band: 15 reps

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 5,000-6,000 steps. Easy does it.

---

## Day 7 — Rest Day

Another full rest day. You've earned it. Relax, spend time with family, enjoy your meals, and let your body recover. This is part of the process.

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 4,000-5,000 steps. Just normal daily movement — no need to push it.

---

## Post-Workout Stretching Routine (Do this after every training session — 8-10 minutes)

After you finish your workout, spend a few minutes stretching the major muscle groups you just trained. Hold each stretch for 30-60 seconds per side, breathe deeply, and relax into it. Don't bounce or force anything.

- **Quad Stretch** (standing, pull one heel toward your glutes): 45 seconds per side  
- **Hamstring Stretch** (seated or standing toe touch): 60 seconds  
- **Hip Flexor Stretch** (lunge position, push hips forward): 45 seconds per side  
- **Chest Stretch** (doorway or wall, arm at 90 degrees): 45 seconds per side  
- **Lat Stretch** (reach one arm overhead and lean to the side): 45 seconds per side  
- **Shoulder Stretch** (pull one arm across your chest): 30 seconds per side  
- **Tricep Stretch** (reach one arm overhead, bend at the elbow): 30 seconds per side  
- **Child's Pose** (knees wide, sit back on your heels, arms extended forward): 60 seconds

This helps reduce soreness, improves flexibility over time, and signals to your nervous system that the workout is done.

---

## Training Intensity Guidance

Let's talk about how hard to push. On your big compound lifts — goblet squats, Romanian deadlifts, floor press, rows — leave 1-2 reps in the tank. That means if you think you could do 8 reps, stop at 6. This keeps your form clean, reduces injury risk, and still builds serious strength. You're a beginner, so your nervous system is still learning these movement patterns. We want quality reps, not grinding through ugly ones.

On isolation and accessory work — bicep curls, lateral raises, glute bridges, band work — you can push closer to failure. These movements are safer to take to the limit because they're single-joint and don't load your spine or require as much coordination. On the last set of these exercises, if you hit failure with strict form and you've got a little more in you, you can add 1-2 controlled cheat reps (using a bit of momentum to keep going). But never, ever do this on squats, deadlifts, RDLs, lunges, or any barbell compound movement. Those require perfect form every single rep.

Rest periods matter. On your heavy compound lifts (4-6 reps), rest 2-3 minutes between sets. On everything else, 60-90 seconds is plenty. If you're gasping for air and your form is falling apart, take an extra 30 seconds. Listen to your body.

---

## Sleep and Recovery

You're already doing well with 7-8 hours, which is solid. Let's aim to keep that consistent and maybe nudge it toward the higher end — 7.5-8 hours is the sweet spot for recovery, especially when you're training. Your body repairs muscle, balances hormones, and consolidates all the hard work you're putting in during sleep. It's not optional if you want results.

Since you're training in the evening and having dinner around 9 pm, try to finish eating by 9:30 pm at the latest. Give yourself at least 2 hours between your last meal and bedtime so your digestion doesn't interfere with sleep quality. If you're hungry later, a small glass of warm milk with a pinch of turmeric or haldi doodh works beautifully — it's calming and won't sit heavy in your stomach.

Your evening chai and snack at 6:30 pm is perfect timing, but if you're having chai after that, try to cut it off by 5-6 pm. Caffeine has a half-life of about 5-6 hours, so even if you don't feel wired, it can mess with your deep sleep cycles.

Screen time is a big one. I know it's tough, but try to put your phone away at least 30-45 minutes before bed. If you need to scroll or check messages, switch your phone to night mode or use blue light filters. Even better, replace that time with something calming — reading, light stretching, or just sitting quietly with family.

Keep your bedroom cool and dark. If you're in a warm climate, a fan or AC set to a comfortable temperature makes a huge difference. Blackout curtains or an eye mask can help if there's street light coming in.

Try to go to bed and wake up at roughly the same time every day, even on weekends. Your body loves routine, and a consistent sleep schedule helps regulate your energy, hunger, and recovery.

If your mind is racing at night or you're feeling stressed from work, spend 5-10 minutes doing some deep breathing before bed. Inhale for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale for 6. Do this for a few rounds and you'll notice your nervous system calming down.

Finally, if you're someone who wakes up to use the bathroom at night, try to front-load your water intake earlier in the day. You're already hitting 2L, which is great — just sip more in the morning and afternoon, and taper off after 8 pm.

---

## Let's Get After It

Pooja, this is a strong starting point. You've got everything you need to build strength, change your body composition, and most importantly, build consistency. That struggle with consistency you mentioned? It's going to disappear once you start seeing progress — and trust me, progress comes fast when you're a beginner and you're doing things right.

Stick to the plan, show up for your three sessions, hit your steps, prioritize your sleep, and check in with me regularly. I want to hear how the workouts feel, what's challenging, what's clicking. We'll adjust as we go, but for now, just focus on nailing the basics.

You've got this. Let's build something great.

issues.description: Preferred exercises from onboarding not included.
